body:
Singapore regulates digital assets through one regulator working two statutes, not a patchwork of specialist agencies each covering a slice of the market. That structural fact -- one regulator, two anchor laws, amended and layered over time -- is the throughline of every pillar board on this site for this jurisdiction, and is worth holding in mind before reading any of them.

Who regulates what

The Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) is simultaneously Singapore's central bank and its single integrated financial regulator -- one authority spanning banking, insurance, securities, and payments supervision, with no separate central-banking or securities-regulator counterpart to divide the work. MAS owns the entire digital-asset perimeter: it licenses and supervises digital payment token (DPT) service providers under the Payment Services Act 2019 (the DPT licensing regime, in force since 28 January 2020 and expanded in stages beginning 4 April 2024 to bring DPT custody, transmission-facilitation, and exchange-facilitation services within its scope), and it holds the omnibus powers created by the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022, under which the Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) regime -- licensing providers who deal in, custody, or advise on digital tokens from Singapore solely for customers located outside Singapore -- has been in force since 30 June 2025. Outside MAS's licensing and conduct remit, the Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore (IRAS) handles the tax side only: supplies of digital payment tokens have been exempt from Goods and Services Tax since 1 January 2020. That is the entire regulatory cast for this jurisdiction. It is a deliberate contrast worth drawing against a jurisdiction like the UAE, where four separate regulators (VARA, the Central Bank, the Securities and Commodities Authority, and the Dubai Financial Services Authority) divide the same ground by emirate and activity type; Singapore instead concentrates licensing, conduct, prudential, and increasingly AML/CFT authority for virtually all digital-asset activity in one institution.

How this jurisdiction's content is curated

Unlike every other live jurisdiction on this site, Singapore has no automated daily feed behind its content. MAS's own website returns an identical, genuine-looking HTTP 200 "Maintenance" error page to any client that does not present as a real browser -- verified directly by this project across MAS's press-release listing, RSS-style paths, sitemap, and several specific document URLs, all serving the same bot-managed shell rather than the requested content. This project's fetch discipline (set out in CLAUDE.md: an honestly-identified, polite client is the compliance strategy, not evasion) means it chose not to disguise its watcher as a browser in order to get past that block. Singapore's content on this site is therefore curated through periodic manual review of MAS's publications, rather than the live daily poll that drives Hong Kong's pages -- so a new MAS action can appear here with a lag that this site's other jurisdictions do not have. A compliance reader deserves to know that plainly, here, not in a footnote. There is a real verification consequence too, and it is worth being equally direct about: where a card cites an MAS media release that is also mirrored, as a genuine PDF, on the Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg) -- the Singapore Government's official press-release repository -- this project's verifier can actually re-fetch that document and character-check the card's claims against it. Where a fact exists only on an mas.gov.sg page that the pipeline cannot fetch at all, the card carries "unverified" status for exactly that reason: the status reflects what this pipeline can machine re-verify, not any doubt about whether MAS actually said it.

The structural throughline

Singapore's digital-asset regime sits on two statutes, not one and not many: the Payment Services Act 2019 and the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022, both administered by MAS and both extended over time by amendment and by subsidiary legislation (regulations, notices, and guidelines made under powers the two Acts confer) rather than by standalone crypto-specific statutes. That places Singapore closer to a single-anchor-statute pattern than to Japan or Switzerland's approach of amending several pre-existing laws in parallel -- but the practical texture is still complex, because MAS has repeatedly built new obligations into the same two Acts through staged commencement dates rather than a single effective date per amendment. The DPT licensing regime itself commenced 28 January 2020, but the custody, transmission-facilitation, and exchange-facilitation duties layered onto it took effect only "in stages from 4 April 2024," with the associated customer-asset-safeguarding regulations following six months later, from 4 October 2024, and the DTSP regime commencing separately, under the other Act, on 30 June 2025. Reading a single "in force since" date for this jurisdiction's DPT regime without checking which specific obligation that date attaches to is this jurisdiction's precision trap, and the pillar boards are written to keep those staged dates distinct rather than collapsing them into one headline date.

In force now, and what is still evolving

The DPT licensing regime under the Payment Services Act, its staged 2024 custody and asset-safeguarding expansions, and the Financial Services and Markets Act's DTSP regime for overseas-only digital token service providers (in force since 30 June 2025) are all current law. The one significant regime still short of legal effect is the dedicated stablecoin framework: MAS announced its finalised design -- reserve-asset value stability, minimum base capital, redemption at par within five business days, and an opt-in "MAS-regulated stablecoin" label -- on 15 August 2023, but that announcement was, by MAS's own description, a completed policy design awaiting the legislative amendments needed to give it legal force. As of this page's writing (13 July 2026), no bill implementing that framework has been introduced in Parliament; secondary reporting has attributed a mid-2026 target for legislative effect to MAS's Managing Director, but that has not been independently confirmed against a primary MAS statement, and readers should treat the stablecoin framework's status as live-evolving rather than settled at the moment they read this page -- check the Stablecoins pillar board for the current position.

The notable feature

Singapore's Payment Services Act DPT regime, commenced 28 January 2020, is arguably the earliest comprehensive digital-payment-token licensing regime among the jurisdictions this site covers -- a full licensing framework for exchanges and related services years before comparable comprehensive regimes existed elsewhere. Alongside that licensing perimeter, MAS runs two long-running exploratory workstreams that sit outside the licensing regime itself: Project Guardian, MAS's industry-collaboration programme on asset tokenisation (tokenised funds, fixed income, and cross-border settlement pilots with dozens of financial institutions), and Project Orchid, MAS's own multi-year programme on the technical infrastructure for digital money, including wholesale central bank digital currency trials. Neither project is itself a licensing regime; both are policy and pilot activity that the pillar boards track as context for where MAS's thinking is heading, distinct from what is currently in force.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas for Singapore in one place. The trajectory board lists officially announced dates and windows -- including the stablecoin framework's pending legislative timeline above -- together. The document library indexes every primary-source document this project has reviewed, whether found through manual curation or, where a document happens to be mirrored on a genuinely fetchable Singapore Government domain such as sgpc.gov.sg or iras.gov.sg, through direct fetch. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout, and terms shared with other jurisdictions link to a single cross-jurisdiction entry. Every summary card cites the specific primary source it is drawn from, and states whether that source could be machine re-verified.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
